items.title: I Canceled My French Tutor and Built an LLM Tool That Does It Better
items.why_it_matters: An indie developer built and launched a custom LLM-based language tutor to replace their human tutor, proving that simple, single-purpose AI wrappers can solve real personal problems. Since you are currently building AI-ingest to solve your own information overload, this is a direct reminder that building simple tools for yourself is the fastest path to shipping. This week, look at how they structured their prompt loops and write a simple system prompt in your Next.js app to handle your AI-ingest filtering logic.

items.title: Show HN: ANMA, boundary contracts for cheaper AI coding agents
items.why_it_matters: ANMA uses YAML contracts and hooks to force cheaper models (like Haiku) to strictly follow your codebase rules, preventing them from breaking your architecture. This is highly relevant if you want to use cheaper models for coding tasks without sacrificing quality. This week, write a basic ANMA contract for your AI-ingest repository to ensure Claude doesn't mess up your Next.js folder structure when writing new features.

items.title: Norrin – Git/ diff control in Claude Code
items.why_it_matters: Norrin adds inline diff control, file tracking, and accept/reject capabilities directly to Claude Code, giving you Cursor-like control over your agent's changes. Since you are currently learning Git discipline on AI-ingest, this tool keeps your commits clean and prevents you from getting lost in massive AI-generated code dumps. This week, install Norrin and use it to review and commit your next feature branch on AI-ingest.

items.title: Show HN: Recall – Local project memory for Claude Code
items.why_it_matters: Recall provides local project memory for Claude Code, helping the agent retain context across sessions without wasting tokens. This makes your daily development smoother by preventing Claude from forgetting how your Next.js and Supabase setup is structured. This week, run Recall on your AI-ingest project directory to see if it reduces the number of times you have to re-explain your database schema to Claude.

items.title: Show HN: Claude Code skills that encode a staff engineer's setup, not prompts
items.why_it_matters: This project shares pre-configured "skills" that encode staff engineer workflows directly into Claude Code, moving beyond basic text prompts. This helps you improve the "direction" part of vibe coding by teaching Claude how to write cleaner, production-ready code. This week, download one of these Next.js or Supabase skill configurations and load it into Claude Code before your next building session.

items.title: Show HN: Crespo – Tree-sitter AST blueprints instead of raw code for LLMs
items.why_it_matters: Crespo converts your codebase into tree-sitter AST blueprints instead of raw text, allowing you to feed your project structure to LLMs while saving massive amounts of token space. This is a highly efficient way to give Claude full context of your Next.js app without blowing through your API budget. This week, run Crespo on your AI-ingest repository and paste the generated blueprint into Claude to ask for architectural feedback.

items.title: Show HN: Lupen – an itemized, verified receipt for Claude Code and Codex spend
items.why_it_matters: Lupen generates an itemized, verified receipt of your Claude Code and Codex spend, showing you exactly which files and prompts are costing you the most money. This is essential for keeping your vibe-coding hobby sustainable as you scale up your build sessions. This week, run Lupen against your local Claude Code logs to see exactly how much it cost to build your latest AI-ingest features.

items.title: Tokoscope – Automatic LLM token compression and cost monitoring in 2 lines
items.why_it_matters: Tokoscope lets you add automatic LLM token compression and cost monitoring to your application with just two lines of code. This is perfect for when you start making API calls from your Next.js backend and want to keep your Supabase and Vercel hosting costs low. This week, install the Tokoscope package in your AI-ingest project to monitor the token usage of your filtering pipeline.
